paragraphs.body: The language I know is Volleyball. Learning this language took time and I truly did not understand it until I was learning the language as a player rather than a NCAA live stream viewer. I started my volleyball career with YouTube videos where I practiced simple exercises along the walls of my basement. I was learning terms from those videos and the live stream college women’s volleyball games I was watching. I quickly fell in love with the sport but due to the pandemic, I wasn’t sure I would get the chance to actually play. I did not start understanding the complexity of the volleyball language until I attended volleyball clinics where I finally had my first team and first volleyball coach. I quickly realized that the volleyball world had a secret language, one that up until that point was speaking incorrectly. I started as a middle, but realized I fit better as an outside hitter. From there, I let myself get swept up in the game. All I wanted to do was play volleyball, anywhere and anytime.
To play volleyball is to sacrifice.

Sacrifice more than your body or time but your mind, You have to stop thinking to win. The only times I forget how secretive volleybal is as a language is when I am surrounded by people who do not play nor watch the sport. It probably sounds foreign to them. With people who are in the volleyball world, its just as if I’m speaking normal english. I love being able to communicate in volleyball with other players at tryouts, with some parents whose kids were on my team or all the volleyball coaches I’ve had the privilage to learn under. Especially on the court while I am playing, with my teammates, it comes out more fluently than regular English. Like I will start screaming some abbreviated phrases that would make no sense in other context. For example, I would scream “TIP, TIP, TIP” when I notice the setter on the other side about to setter dump or “COVER DEEP” when we we’re in receive and would have to cover the back row or “FOUR FOUR” when I ask my setter to set me that specific set so I can get an outside shot that I think will win us that point.

When I was captain of my volleyball club teams, I was an all-around player which mean’t I was never sub’d (substituted) of the court. Naturally, when I fell in love with this sport, I craved reaching the next level. I just wanted to keep improving, keep challenging myself and ultimately when I was first given the opportunity as captain I felt so much pride. I saw it as an acknowledgement of all my work. Being captain came with new responsibilities that I did not expect and on top of those that already came with being an all-around player. As an all-around player, I was constantly prioritizing my health by watching my diet, stretching, running, etc. because I knew I needed to maintain my stamina for multiple sets. As captain, my leadership and communication skills were strengthened, as I had to help resolve conflicts between my teammates. Most of the time, I was working with players who had big personalities, so it was challenging but I learned a lot of valuable lessons. The accomplishment I am most proud of is the tenacity I built by playing this sport. In my junior year of hghschool, I tried out for my school’s varsity team and made it to the last day of tryouts until I was cut. Coach Jackie told me “At first I didn’t have you on my radar but you have improved so much. I had to switch my list around but unfortunately, I am on the bubble about you. I don’t know where to put you”. That was enough for me to wake up the next morning and immediately start training. I went to the gym and with whatever space I could find, I was practicing. I was recording myself to watch back and practice proper form. I was asking for advice from old coaches. I was not giving up. Next year, I made the Varisty team. Finally. I felt so strong and so proud of myself.

It is unfortunate that the volleyball world was the only place I felt like this in. I view the volleyball community as inclusive and equal. A space where I felt safe to be as aggressive, competitive, loud, and energetic as I could be. It was encouraged because the teams with those types of players were the ones going home with gold medals. As a women of color, those exact adjectives were stereotypes that I grew up being afraid of. Those were the adjectives that gave reputations like “bad kids” or “ghetto kids” and those were the last labels I would ever want. I grew up in the suburbs, so the from my earliest memories I have lived in predominantly white areas. To anyone that can relate to being one of the three or four minorities living in these areas, you can understand the negative impact taken on your self-esteem and confidence. Not only that but also your perspective on your own community. I remember wanted to distant myself from the community I am apart of just because I wanted to assimiulated with my classmates easily. I wanted to “fit-in” because my fear was being alienated. I did everything I could from buying trending clothing brands to damaging my curly hair with straightners every week to hiding during the summer so my skin wouldn’t get dark. I built my comfort zone around what my white classmates felt comfortable with. All because I wanted to fit in with a group that will never view me as equal to them, wether they concioussly choose to or subconciously do because I grew up convinced that “white was right”.

To grow up in the suburbs it to have a white voice Even though a voice doesn’t, excuse me, DOES NOT have a color As I’ve gotten older and seen the beauty of differrent minority communities, I feel embarresed to have tried so hard to change my identiy. I do owe much of that realization to volleyball. In the game, I am simply a player, equal to all the other players on the court, working to achieve the simple goal of winning the set. Race wasn’t a factor. Gender wasn’t a factor. Or at least, it never felt like it which I am so grateful to have experienced such a diverse space. Everytime I walked on the court, I didn’t have to feel like my looks would affect the outcome or would alienate me. Every accomplishment I earned in volleyball, I earned with hard work and no one can deny that from me because of racism or sexism. I walked on the court feeling impowered. I felt like I belonged. Like I had always belonged. They didn’t tell me I was “good for a girl” or “good for a girl of color” they told me I was great. “Carol Everly is a great player.” Simple.

As a woman of color, I know in the real world there will always be people trying to limit my success just because of my apparence. But, with the impowerment I gained from volleyball, I will continue to work hard and stay tenacious until I achieve what I aim for. Defying the limits others will put on me, is a small step in the overall fight for equality. Becoming so good at what I set my mind to, becoming so powerful that no one can deminish my accomplishments or make me feel like an outsider.

To women, especially women of color, you and I have no choice but to continue fighting. Find a safe space where you feel you belong. It changed my life, my perspective, and my overall character forever. Thank you volleyball for giving me the push out of my comfort zone that I needed.

annotations.comment: As we discussed in our email conversation, due to course policies concerning deadlines and the final portfolio, the original grade for this essay will remain as originally assigned.
